1: Isaiah 46:1 opens the taunt against Babylon's gods with their humiliation: 'kar Bel kara Nevo = Bel bows down; Nebo stoops.' Bel (= Marduk = the chief god of Babylon; Bel means 'lord' = the same Semitic root as the Canaanite Baal; Marduk was identified with Enlil and called Bel = his great temple Esagila with its ziggurat Etemenanki was one of the wonders of the ANE). Nebo (= Nabu = the scribal god, patron of literacy and wisdom, son of Marduk; his great temple Ezida was at Borsippa, visible from Babylon). Both names appear in Babylonian royal names: Nebuchadnezzar (Nabu-kudurri-usur), Nabonidus (Nabu-na'id), Belshazzar (Bel-shar-usur). 'Their idols are loaded onto animals and cattle; the things you bear are burdens on exhausted beasts' — in the Babylonian Akitu (New Year) festival, the cult-statues of Bel and Nebo were carried in procession through Babylon on elaborately decorated sledges pulled by oxen. When Cyrus conquered Babylon, these same gods were looted — the procession-statues became war-plunder on pack animals.
2: 'They stoop and bow down together; they cannot save the load — they themselves go into captivity' — the double humiliation of Babylon's gods: 'qaresu kare'u yachdav lo yakhellu mallit massah = they stooped and bowed down together; they could not save/deliver the burden/load.' The gods who were supposed to protect Babylon cannot even protect themselves — they are 'the burden' (massah = the carried load) on the pack animals, and they cannot 'deliver the burden' (mallit massah = save the load = they themselves are the cargo that cannot save itself). 'They themselves go into captivity' (venaphsham bashebi halakha = their own souls/selves went into captivity) — the gods of Babylon are taken captive when Babylon falls. The theology: when Babylon was defeated, the Babylonians would naturally interpret this as their gods' defeat. Isaiah agrees: the gods have gone into captivity. But YHWH has not been defeated — he orchestrated the entire event.
3: 'Listen to me, O house of Jacob, and all the remnant of the house of Israel, you who have been carried since before birth, borne since the womb' — the contrast with the idol-gods who need carrying: 'shim'u elai beit-Ya'aqov vekhol she'erit beit Yisrael = listen to me, house of Jacob, and all the remnant of the house of Israel.' The she'erit (remnant = the theologically loaded term for the survivors of divine judgment who carry the covenant forward; Isaiah 10:20-22; Isaiah 37:31-32). 'You who have been carried since before birth' (hannessuim min-beten = the ones carried from the belly/womb) — the participle hannessuim (carried ones) = Israel as the ones YHWH has been carrying from before birth. The contrast with Bel and Nebo: the Babylonian gods need to be carried by animals; YHWH has been carrying Israel since before they were born.
4: 'Even to your old age I am he, and even to gray hairs I will carry you. I have made you, and I will sustain you; I will carry you and save you' — YHWH's life-span commitment: 've'ad-ziknah ani hu ve'ad-sevah ani esbol = and to old age I am he, and to gray hairs I will carry.' The four-fold divine promise: (1) 'I am he' (ani hu = the divine self-identification of Isaiah 41:4; Isaiah 43:10-13; Isaiah 48:12); (2) 'I will carry' (esbol = from saval = to carry as a burden, to sustain under weight; the heaviest kind of carrying); (3) 'I have made you' (ani asiti); (4) 'I will sustain and carry and save' (va'ani essa va'ani esbol va'amallet = I will carry and I will sustain and I will deliver/save). The life-span: from womb (v3) to old age/gray hairs (v4) = YHWH carries Israel through the entire arc of existence. The four-fold chiasm of carrying concludes with deliverance — the entire carrying-project has salvation as its end.
5: 'To whom will you compare me and make me equal, and liken me, that we may be alike?' — the incomparability question mid-chapter: 'le-mi tedammeyuni vetashvu = to whom will you liken me and make me equal?' The three comparison-terms: (1) damah (liken); (2) shavah (make equal); (3) mashal (compare). The 'that we may be alike' (venidammeh = and we will be compared/equal) — the invitation to find a divine category-companion for YHWH. The question resumes the incomparability section of Isaiah 40:18-26 after the Babylon-god-humiliation of vv1-4. The contrast: the gods that are carried vs. YHWH who carries; the gods who go into captivity vs. YHWH who redeems from captivity. No comparison is possible.
6: 'Those who pour out gold from their purses and weigh out silver in the scales hire a goldsmith, who makes it into a god; then they fall down and worship it' — the wealthy man's idol-making: 'hazzelim zahav mikkis vekhesef baqaneh yishqqolu = those who lavish gold from the purse and silver they weigh in the scales.' The careful weighing of precious metals (the ancient payment system before coinage) for the idol-commission. 'Hire a goldsmith, who makes it into a god' (yiskeru tsaref veyesuhu El = they hire a goldsmith and he makes it into a god). The commercial transaction that produces a 'god': the idol is literally purchased. 'Then they fall down and worship it' (af-yishtachabu af-yisgadu = also they bow and also they prostrate). The progression: careful financial investment → commissioned manufacture → worship of the purchased object. The economic absurdity is complete: wealth spent on creating an object that is then worshiped as if it created wealth.
7: 'They hoist it onto their shoulder, they carry it and set it in its place, and there it stands; it cannot move from its spot. If someone cries out to it, it gives no answer and rescues no one from trouble' — the four failures of the purchased god: (1) 'yissahuhu al-khatef yisvuluhu = they lift it on the shoulder, they carry it' — the god cannot transport itself; (2) 'vayannichuhw tachatav vaya'amod = and they set it down under it and it stands' — the god cannot stand up by itself; (3) 'lo-yamus mimmaqomo = it cannot move from its spot' — the god cannot move at all; (4) 'af-yitsa'aq elav velo ya'aneh = also one cries out to it and it does not answer; mitsaratho lo yoshiannu = from his trouble he does not save him' — the idol cannot hear prayer and cannot deliver from trouble (the primary test of deity: saving). 1 Kings 18:26: 'Baal! Baal!' — no response, no voice, no answer.
8: 'Remember this and stand firm; bring it to mind, you rebels' — the summons to memory and stability: 'zikhru-zot vehitashoshashu = remember this and stand firm/be resolute.' The hitashoshashu (stand firm = from asheshah = to be firm, confirmed; an unusual word perhaps from esh = fire = 'be fired up/steadfast'). 'Bring it to mind, you rebels' (hashivu al-lev posh'im = bring it back to your heart, O transgressors/rebels). The posh'im (transgressors/rebels = from pasha = to rebel, the covenant violation term of Isaiah 1:2; Isaiah 43:27; Isaiah 48:8) addressed as the audience: even the rebels among Israel are summoned to remember. The 'this' (zot = this) that must be remembered = the entire argument of vv1-7: the idol-gods go into captivity while YHWH carries Israel.
9: 'Remember the former things of long ago; for I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like me' — the dual incomparability statement: 'zikhru rishonut me'olam = remember the former things from antiquity.' The rishonut (former things = the saving acts of YHWH in primordial history: the Exodus, the wilderness, the conquest) as the evidence-base for trust. 'For I am God, and there is no other' (ki anoki El ve'en od = for I am God and there is no other) and 'I am God, and there is none like me' (Elohim ve'efes kamooni = God and there is no one like me). The two complementary claims: (1) there is no other in the divine category (en od = none besides); (2) there is no one similar to me (efes kamooni = there is no equivalent/likeness of me). Both claims together: no other deity exists, and even hypothetically no such being could resemble YHWH.
10: 'Declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things not yet done, saying, My plan shall stand, and I will carry out my purpose' — YHWH's unique predictive capacity: 'maggid mere'shit acharit = declaring from the beginning the end.' The beginning-to-end prediction: YHWH knows and declares the acharit (end/outcome) from the re'shit (beginning = the very first moment). This is not merely prediction but the declaration of the divine plan from its inception. 'From ancient times things not yet done' (umiqedem asher lo-naastu = and from antiquity things not yet done) — before the events happen, YHWH announces them. 'Saying, My plan shall stand, and I will carry out my purpose' (omer atsati taqum vekhol-chefetsi e'eseh = saying: my counsel will stand and all my pleasure I will do). The atsah (plan/counsel = the divine plan; Isaiah 5:19: 'his plan draws near'; Isaiah 25:1: 'your counsel of old is faithful and true') that stands unalterably.
11: 'Calling a bird of prey from the east, the man of my purpose from a far country. I have spoken, and I will bring it about; I have planned, and I will do it' — the Cyrus-bird oracle: 'qore miqqedem ayit = calling from the east a bird of prey.' The ayit (bird of prey = a swift-flying predatory bird; possibly an eagle, hawk, or vulture) from the east = Cyrus, whose campaigns swept from the east with remarkable speed (the eagle-speed of military movement: Deuteronomy 28:49: 'a nation that swoops down on you like an eagle'). 'The man of my purpose from a far country' (ish atsati me'erets merchaq = the man of my counsel from a far land) = Cyrus as the embodiment of YHWH's atsah (plan/purpose). 'I have spoken, and I will bring it about; I have planned, and I will do it' (af-dibarti af-avi'ennah yatsarti af-e'esennah = indeed I spoke, indeed I will bring it; I formed/planned, indeed I will do it) — the divine word-to-act sequence: speech precedes and guarantees action.
12: 'Listen to me, you stubborn of heart, you who are far from righteousness' — the appeal to the hardened exiles: 'shim'u elai abirei-lev = listen to me, you mighty-of-heart/stubborn of heart.' The abirei-lev (mighty ones of heart = the stiff-hearted, those whose inner resolve is hardened against divine appeal; from abbir = mighty, strong) — the 'stubborn of heart' is not a neutral strength but the obstinacy of those who resist YHWH's persuasion. 'Who are far from righteousness' (harecho'kim mittsedaqah = the far ones from righteousness) — tsedaqah as the covenant right-ordering that defines the proper relationship with YHWH: these are the exiles who have concluded that YHWH has abandoned them or cannot fulfill his promises, and so have positioned themselves far from him in their hearts.
13: 'I am bringing my righteousness near — it is not far off — and my salvation will not linger; I will put salvation in Zion, for Israel my glory' — the answer to the 'far from righteousness' of v12: 'qaravti tsidqati lo tirchaq = I have brought near my righteousness, it will not be far.' The reversal: those far from righteousness (v12) find YHWH's righteousness coming near to them. 'My salvation will not linger' (uteshu'ati lo te'acheer = and my salvation will not delay). 'I will put salvation in Zion' (venatatti beVTsiyon teshu'ah = and I will give in Zion salvation) — Zion as the place where divine salvation is established and from which it radiates (cf. Isaiah 2:3: 'from Zion shall go forth the law, and from Jerusalem the word of YHWH'). 'For Israel my glory' (leYisrael tif'arti = for Israel my splendor/beauty/glory) — Israel as YHWH's tif'arah (the beautiful thing in which he takes pride and displays his character; Isaiah 44:23: 'displays his glory in Israel').
